figures.caption: Fig. 4: UMAP visualization of learned representations. Embeddings for OrganMNIST3D across batch sizes (rows) and methods (columns). IConE (leftmost column, green border) maintains well-separated class clusters at all batch sizes, while batch-dependent methods show progressive collapse as batch size decreases.

Fig. 7: Per-dataset 2D results. Linear probe balanced accuracy vs. batch size for each 2D MedMNIST dataset. In most datasets, IConE maintains consistent performance across all batch sizes, while other baselines show characteristic degradation patterns at small batch sizes.

Fig. 13: Representation quality metrics on 3D MedMNIST datasets. Same format as Fig. 12. IConE’s advantages are even more pronounced in 3D, where the increased computational cost of volumetric data makes small-batch training essential. At B = 1 and B = 2, baseline methods show near-complete dimensional collapse (low RankMe, LiDAR, Effective Rank), while IConE maintains high-quality representations with good dimensional utilization.

Fig. 12: Representation quality metrics on 2D MedMNIST datasets. Each row shows a different dataset; each column shows a different metric as a function of batch size. Higher is better for RankMe, LiDAR, and Effective Rank (indicating better dimensional utilization). Lower is better for Lalign (tighter same-class clusters) and Luniform (more uniform coverage). IConE (green) consistently achieves the best collapse-related metrics while maintaining competitive alignment and uniformity, especially at small batch sizes where baseline methods exhibit severe collapse.

Fig. 8: Per-dataset 3D results. Linear probe balanced accuracy vs. batch size for each 3D MedMNIST dataset. IConE’s batch-agnostic design provides consistent advantages across all volumetric imaging tasks.
Fig. 2: Batch-size stability. Mean and standard deviation linear-probe top-1 balanced accuracy aggregated over (A) 2D datasets and (B) 3D datasets.

Fig. 3: Batch sensitivity analysis. Left: Performance drop from largest to smallest batch size (lower is better). Right: Correlation between batch size and performance versus absolute top-1 balanced accuracy. IConE occupies the desirable top-left region: high performance with minimal batch dependence.

Fig. 9: Joint batch size and dataset size analysis. Balanced accuracy heatmaps for dataset sizes N ∈ {500, 1k, 2k, 5k}. IConE maintains stable high performance (dark blue) across all conditions, while baselines degrade significantly at small batch sizes, particularly in data-scarce regimes.

Fig. 6: Robustness to class imbalance. (A) Overall top-1 balanced accuracy vs. imbalance ratio. (B) Minority class accuracy vs. imbalance ratio. IConE maintains superior performance on both metrics, with particularly strong advantages on minority classes at high imbalance ratios. Results are averaged over all 2D and 3D datasets.

Fig. 1: Decoupling invariance and anti-collapse gradients with IConE. Left: Standard JEAs apply both the multi-view invariance objective (Linvariance) and the anti-collapse mechanism (Lregularization) directly within the encoder’s dynamic representation space Z. This couples the anti-collapse constraint to the current batch. Right: IConE transfers the anti-collapse mechanism to an explicitly different parameter space. A persistent embedding table yields instance-specific anchors E, which are independently structured via Lregularization. The encoder f is optimized purely through attractive forces: local view consistency (Linvariance) and alignment to its regularized target (Lalignment). This structural decoupling removes the need for batch-dependent negative sampling or variance statistics, applying the repulsive force to the entire dataset at the same time.

Fig. 16: Classification accuracy during training. (a) kNN-5 accuracy. (b) Linear probe accuracy. Full IConE maintains stable high performance, while ablated variants show degraded or unstable learning.

Fig. 17: Alignment and uniformity metrics [39] during training. (a) Alignment loss (lower = tighter same-class clusters). (b) Uniformity loss (lower = more spread). Full IConE achieves the best trade-off with both low alignment and low uniformity. Without Lvv , alignment suffers; without Ldiv , uniformity suffers.

Fig. 5: Representation geometry analysis. (A) Alignment vs. uniformity loss for 3D datasets, colored by downstream accuracy. (B) Accuracy vs. effective rank (3D). (C) RankMe vs. batch size (2D). (D) LiDAR vs. batch size (2D). IConE achieves superior geometry metrics across all measures.

Fig. 15: Training evolution across all ablation variants. Each row shows a different variant; each column shows representations at a specific epoch (0, 5, 25, 100, 200, 299). Points are colored by class label.
